figures.plain_language: The Transformer, in one picture. The left tower is the encoder — it reads the input sentence. Six identical blocks are stacked; each one lets every word attend to every other (self-attention), then passes each word's vector through a small neural network. The right tower is the decoder — it produces the output, one word at a time. Its blocks do two attention steps per layer: one over the words it's generated so far (masked, so it can't peek at future words), and one over the encoder's output — this is where input meets output. At the very top, a linear layer plus softmax turns the decoder's final state into a probability distribution over the next output word.

The core attention operation in one picture. Q is a stack of query vectors, K of keys, V of values. Step 1: MatMul of Q·K^T gives a matrix of scores — how much each query “matches” each key, via a dot product. Step 2: Scale divides by √d_k so softmax doesn't saturate at large d_k. Step 3: Mask (used only in the decoder) zeroes out positions we're not allowed to attend to. Step 4: Softmax turns each row of scores into a probability distribution. Step 5: MatMul with V gives the output — each position becomes a weighted mix of values, weights set by the softmax.

Multi-head attention stacks eight of the operation above in parallel. Each input stream (Q, K, V) is first projected through its own small linear layer, so each of the eight heads sees a different 64-dimensional view of the same data. Each head then runs the scaled-dot-product attention on its view. The eight outputs are concatenated and passed through a final linear projection that collapses them back to 512 dimensions. Same total compute as one head at full width, but eight independent attention patterns instead of one averaged one.

claims.statement: Recurrent neural networks, long short-term memory and gated recurrent neural networks in particular, have been firmly established as state of the art approaches in sequence modeling and transduction problems such as language modeling and machine translation.
claims.passage: Recurrent neural networks, long short-term memory and gated recurrent neural networks in particular, have been firmly established as state of the art approaches in sequence modeling and transduction problems such as language modeling and machine translation
claims.plain_language: Before this paper landed in 2017, if you wanted a model to read or translate text, you reached for a recurrent network — an LSTM or a GRU. They'd been the workhorses of the field for years, and they were genuinely good at their jobs.

claims.statement: The inherently sequential nature of recurrent models precludes parallelization within training examples, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths.
claims.passage: This inherently sequential nature precludes parallelization within training examples, which becomes critical at longer sequence lengths, as memory constraints limit batching across examples
claims.plain_language: But recurrent networks had a fundamental problem: they read a sentence strictly word-by-word. Word 1, then word 2, then word 3. Each step had to wait for the one before. Even with a hundred GPUs sitting idle, you couldn't speed training up within a single sentence. And the longer the sentence, the more this hurt.

claims.statement: We propose the Transformer, a model architecture eschewing recurrence and instead relying entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output.
claims.passage: we propose the Transformer, a model architecture eschewing recurrence and instead relying entirely on an attention mechanism to draw global dependencies between input and output
claims.plain_language: The authors did something bold: they threw recurrence out entirely. The Transformer reads every word at once, in parallel, and uses a mechanism called *attention* — a way for each word to look directly at every other word — to handle the relationships between them.

claims.statement: The Transformer allows for significantly more parallelization and can reach a new state of the art in translation quality after being trained for as little as twelve hours on eight P100 GPUs.
claims.passage: The Transformer allows for significantly more parallelization and can reach a new state of the art in translation quality after being trained for as little as twelve hours on eight P100 GPUs
claims.plain_language: That one change made training dramatically faster. On eight GPUs, a fresh Transformer reached a *new* state of the art for translation in roughly twelve hours. The previous best translation models took days or weeks on bigger hardware.

claims.statement: In convolutional sequence models (Extended Neural GPU, ByteNet, ConvS2S), the number of operations required to relate signals from two arbitrary positions grows with their distance — linearly for ConvS2S and logarithmically for ByteNet — making it more difficult to learn long-range dependencies.
claims.passage: the number of operations required to relate signals from two arbitrary input or output positions grows in the distance between positions, linearly for ConvS2S and logarithmically for ByteNet. This makes it more difficult to learn dependencies between distant positions
claims.plain_language: Earlier attempts to replace RNNs — like ByteNet and ConvS2S — used convolutions instead. Those parallelized nicely, but they inherited a different problem: it took more compute to relate two words the further apart they were. Connecting the first word of a long sentence to the last was genuinely hard. The Transformer's attention fixes this — the cost of relating word 1 to word 500 is the same as relating word 1 to word 2.

claims.statement: The encoder is composed of a stack of N=6 identical layers; each layer has two sub-layers — a multi-head self-attention mechanism and a position-wise fully connected feed-forward network — with a residual connection and layer normalization around each.
claims.passage: The encoder is composed of a stack of \(N=6\) identical layers. Each layer has two sub-layers. The first is a multi-head self-attention mechanism, and the second is a simple, position-wise fully connected feed-forward network
claims.plain_language: The encoder — the half of the model that reads the input — is six identical blocks in a stack. Each block does two things in order: first every word attends to every other word (self-attention), then each word's vector is passed through a small neural network (the feed-forward layer). Residual “skip” connections wrap around each step, so the raw information can always pass through unchanged if the block doesn't want to modify it.

claims.statement: The decoder modifies its self-attention sub-layer to prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions, so that the prediction for position i depends only on outputs at earlier positions.
claims.passage: We also modify the self-attention sub-layer in the decoder stack to prevent positions from attending to subsequent positions
claims.plain_language: The decoder — the half that generates the translation — has an important twist. When it's deciding what word to produce next, we hide the future: it's not allowed to peek at words it hasn't produced yet. This is enforced by zeroing out the attention scores for later positions, so the model literally cannot see them.

claims.statement: We compute the attention as dot products of queries with keys, scale by 1/sqrt(d_k), and apply a softmax to obtain the weights over values.
claims.passage: We compute the dot products of the query with all keys, divide each by \(\sqrt{d_k}\) , and apply a softmax function to obtain the weights on the values
claims.plain_language: Here's what attention actually does, in three concrete steps. Each word is projected into three vectors: a *query* (“what am I looking for?”), a *key* (“what do I represent?”), and a *value* (“what do I contribute?”). (1) To compute attention for the word “the,” you take its query and dot-product it against every other word's key — the bigger the dot product, the more relevant. (2) Divide by √d_k, run through softmax, and now the scores sum to 1 — you've got a probability distribution over every word. (3) Use those probabilities to take a weighted average of the other words' values. That average is “the”'s new vector. Intuition: if “the” is looking for its antecedent in “the cat sat,” its query lines up strongly with “cat”'s key — so “the” ends up mostly mixing in “cat”'s value.

claims.statement: We suspect that for large values of d_k the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax into regions with extremely small gradients; we scale by 1/sqrt(d_k) to counteract this.
claims.passage: We suspect that for large values of \(d_k\) , the dot products grow large in magnitude, pushing the softmax function into regions where it has extremely small gradients
claims.plain_language: Why divide the scores by √d_k? When the key vectors are high-dimensional (say d_k = 64), the raw dot products tend to come out *large in magnitude*. Large scores push softmax into a saturated regime where one score dominates and the gradients become nearly zero — so nothing can learn. Dividing by √d_k (so √64 = 8) shrinks the scores back into a range where softmax still has usable gradients. A small fix, but without it this architecture wouldn't train.

claims.statement: Multi-head attention lets the model jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions; a single attention head averages this away.
claims.passage: Multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. With a single attention head, averaging inhibits this
claims.plain_language: Rather than running one big attention operation, the Transformer runs eight smaller ones *in parallel* — these are the “heads” — and stitches them together. Why? Because any single attention head has to pick *one* pattern of what-looks-at-what. With eight heads, the model can learn eight different patterns at once: maybe one head tracks subject-verb agreement, another tracks the noun a pronoun refers to, another captures topic or tone. One head alone would have to mash all of these patterns into a single fuzzy signal.

claims.statement: We employ h=8 parallel attention heads, each with d_k = d_v = d_model / h = 64, keeping total computational cost similar to single-head attention at full dimensionality.
claims.passage: In this work we employ \(h=8\) parallel attention layers, or heads. For each of these we use \(d_k=d_v=d_{\text{model}}/h=64\)
claims.plain_language: Concretely: eight heads, each working on a 64-dimensional slice of the 512-dimensional representation (8 × 64 = 512). So the total compute is roughly the same as running a single head at the full 512 dimensions — but now you get eight independent attention patterns instead of one averaged one. A nice trade.

claims.statement: Because the model has no recurrence or convolution, we inject positional information via sinusoidal positional encodings added to the input embeddings.
claims.passage: we add "positional encodings" to the input embeddings at the bottoms of the encoder and decoder stacks
claims.plain_language: Here's a subtle problem with pure attention: it has no idea what order the words came in. To an attention layer, “dog bites man” and “man bites dog” look the same — just a set of three tokens. To fix this, the authors add a *position signal* to each word's embedding before the model sees it: a vector whose value depends on where the word sits in the sentence. The specific signal they chose was a mix of sines and cosines at different frequencies — think of a clock with multiple hands, each rotating at its own speed.

claims.statement: We chose the sinusoidal version because it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than those encountered during training.
claims.passage: We chose the sinusoidal version because it may allow the model to extrapolate to sequence lengths longer than the ones encountered during training
claims.plain_language: Why sinusoids specifically? The authors argued: a learned position embedding (one vector per position) runs out the moment you hit a sentence longer than anything in training. But a sinusoidal pattern extends forever, so *in principle* the model should cope with sentences longer than it has ever seen. They hedged this with “may allow” — they weren't sure it would actually work, but it was the cleanest theoretical argument.

claims.statement: A self-attention layer connects all positions with a constant number of sequentially executed operations, whereas a recurrent layer requires O(n) sequential operations.
claims.passage: a self-attention layer connects all positions with a constant number of sequentially executed operations, whereas a recurrent layer requires \(O(n)\) sequential operations
claims.plain_language: Compare how long it takes to connect two words. An RNN reading “The cat that the dog chased was black” has to process every word between “cat” and “black” sequentially before the signal from one can reach the other. Self-attention connects them in a single step — “cat” can look at “black” directly, no matter how many words are in between. On a GPU that can run many operations at once, this difference is huge.

claims.statement: When the sequence length n is smaller than the representation dimension d (the typical case in machine translation with word-piece or byte-pair tokens), self-attention layers are faster than recurrent layers.
claims.passage: self-attention layers are faster than recurrent layers when the sequence length \(n\) is smaller than the representation dimensionality \(d\) , which is most often the case with sentence representations used by state-of-the-art models in machine translations
claims.plain_language: There's a second win on top of the parallelism. For typical translation sentence lengths (tens of words, much smaller than the 512-dimensional representation), the total compute inside a self-attention layer is actually *lower* than inside a recurrent layer of the same size. So self-attention isn't just easier to parallelize — it's often cheaper in absolute terms.

claims.statement: As a side benefit, self-attention could yield more interpretable models; different attention heads appear to specialize, some exhibiting behavior tied to syntactic and semantic structure.
claims.passage: self-attention could yield more interpretable models. We inspect attention distributions from our models and present and discuss examples in the appendix
claims.plain_language: A third, quieter benefit: you can *see* what the model is doing. Each head's attention pattern is a matrix of soft weights — you can plot it as a heatmap. When the authors looked, individual heads had clearly specialized: one head seemed to track which word each pronoun refers to; another attended strongly from verbs to their direct objects. The model was rediscovering the shape of grammar without being told it existed.

claims.statement: We trained on WMT 2014 English-German (~4.5M sentence pairs) and WMT 2014 English-French (36M sentences), using byte-pair / word-piece vocabularies.
claims.passage: We trained on the standard WMT 2014 English-German dataset consisting of about 4.5 million sentence pairs
claims.plain_language: Training data was the standard WMT 2014 corpora — English-German (4.5 million sentence pairs) and English-French (36 million). Sentences were tokenized into byte-pair or word-piece units so that rare words get broken into reusable sub-pieces (so “unbelievable” might become “un-believ-able,” letting the model handle words it's never seen as a whole).

claims.statement: We trained using the Adam optimizer with beta_1 = 0.9, beta_2 = 0.98, epsilon = 1e-9, with a warmup-then-inverse-square-root learning rate schedule.
claims.passage: We used the Adam optimizer
claims.plain_language: The optimizer was Adam with fairly standard settings. The learning rate did something clever though: it *warmed up* linearly over the first 4,000 steps (starting tiny, growing steadily), then decayed as 1/√step after that. Think of it as an accelerator pedal that's eased into before it settles. Warmup is now standard practice for training large Transformers; this paper helped establish the recipe.

claims.statement: Single-head attention is 0.9 BLEU worse than the best multi-head setting, and quality also drops when we use too many heads.
claims.passage: single-head attention is 0.9 BLEU worse than the best setting, quality also drops off with too many heads
claims.plain_language: On the ablation: dropping to a single attention head costs 0.9 BLEU (substantial for translation). Going *past* the sweet spot also hurts. Eight heads turned out to be roughly optimal, but the curve is a U-shape — too few and the model can't capture multiple patterns; too many and each head is too narrow to learn anything useful on its own.

claims.statement: On WMT 2014 English-to-German, the big Transformer outperforms the best previously reported models (including ensembles) by more than 2.0 BLEU, establishing a new state-of-the-art BLEU score of 28.4.
claims.passage: the big transformer model (Transformer (big) in Table 2 ) outperforms the best previously reported models (including ensembles) by more than \(2.0\) BLEU, establishing a new state-of-the-art BLEU score of \(28.4\)
claims.plain_language: The headline result. On English→German translation, the big Transformer beat every prior system — including *ensembles*, which combine multiple models and usually have a big advantage — by more than 2 BLEU points. BLEU 28.4. That was the new state of the art.

claims.statement: Training the big model took 3.5 days on 8 P100 GPUs.
claims.passage: Training took \(3.5\) days on \(8\) P100 GPUs
claims.plain_language: And it took just 3.5 days on 8 GPUs to get there. The previous state-of-the-art model had trained on dozens of GPUs for weeks. Training cost wasn't just a little better — it was roughly an order of magnitude less.

claims.statement: On WMT 2014 English-to-French, the big Transformer achieves 41.0 BLEU, outperforming all previously published single models at less than 1/4 the training cost of the previous state of the art.
claims.passage: our big model achieves a BLEU score of \(41.0\) , outperforming all of the previously published single models, at less than \(1/4\) the training cost of the previous state-of-the-art model
claims.plain_language: On English→French (the other standard benchmark), the big Transformer scored 41.0 BLEU — beating every previously published *single* model (non-ensemble), at less than a quarter of the compute the previous leader had used.

claims.statement: Reducing the attention key size d_k hurts model quality, suggesting that determining compatibility is not easy and that a more sophisticated compatibility function than dot product may be beneficial.
claims.passage: reducing the attention key size \(d_k\) hurts model quality. This suggests that determining compatibility is not easy and that a more sophisticated compatibility function than dot product may be beneficial
claims.plain_language: An interesting ablation: when they shrank the key dimension d_k, translation quality dropped noticeably. Their speculation: plain dot products might be too blunt an instrument for deciding which words should attend to which. A more sophisticated compatibility function — maybe a small neural network scoring queries against keys — could work better. (Nobody's really replaced dot products in practice, but this was an early hint.)

claims.statement: Replacing sinusoidal positional encodings with learned positional embeddings yields nearly identical results.
claims.passage: we replace our sinusoidal positional encoding with learned positional embeddings , and observe nearly identical results to the base model
claims.plain_language: They also tried swapping the sinusoidal position encoding for a learned one — just an embedding table indexed by position — and got nearly identical scores. The sinusoid story might be elegant, but on these benchmarks at these sentence lengths, the model doesn't actually care which scheme you use. Learned position embeddings have become more common in later models.

claims.statement: On English constituency parsing, despite the lack of task-specific tuning the Transformer performs surprisingly well, yielding better results than all previously reported models except the Recurrent Neural Network Grammar.
claims.passage: despite the lack of task-specific tuning our model performs surprisingly well, yielding better results than all previously reported models with the exception of the Recurrent Neural Network Grammar
claims.plain_language: Finally: was this specific to translation? They pointed the same architecture at *English constituency parsing* — producing a grammatical tree for each sentence — with almost no tweaking. It performed surprisingly well, better than nearly every prior system on that task. Good evidence the architecture generalizes beyond the problem it was designed for.

claims.statement: Making generation less sequential is another research goal of ours.
claims.passage: Making generation less sequential is another research goals of ours
claims.plain_language: One loose thread: while *training* is now fully parallel, *generation* still isn't. When the Transformer produces a translation at inference time, it has to produce word 1, then word 2, then word 3 — each conditioned on the previous. Making generation less sequential is flagged as future work. (Several papers since have tried; it's still an open problem at the time of writing.)
